coredata.dc:title: Direct Z-scheme SiNWs@Co3O4 photocathode with a cocatalyst of sludge-derived carbon quantum dots for efficient photoelectrochemical hydrogen production

coredata.dc:description: Solar driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen production has attracted considerable attention, but the design of highly efficient, robust and low-cost photocathode still remains a significant challenge. Herein, we report a novel SiNWs@Co3O4 Z-scheme heterojunction photocathode with carbon quantum dots eco-friendly derived from sludge (SCQDs) as the co-catalyst. The photocathode not only leads to effective separation of electron–hole pair, lower transmission resistance, and longer lifetime of charge carriers, but also elevates the stability by preventing direct contact between the SiNWs and the electrolyte as well as the self-oxidation. Simultaneously, the excellent electron transport properties of the SCQDs further improved the PEC performance. Correspondingly, a maximum current density of 14.88 mA·cm−2 was obtained at −0.67 V with the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) achieving 8.4% under visible light irradiations at pH = 7. This work provides a promising scheme for Si-based photocathodes for PEC hydrogen production with a high performance, reliable stability, and low-cost.

head.ce:title: Direct Z-scheme SiNWs@Co3O4 photocathode with a cocatalyst of sludge-derived carbon quantum dots for efficient photoelectrochemical hydrogen production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen production has attracted considerable attention, but the design of highly efficient, robust and low-cost photocathode still remains a significant challenge. Herein, we report a novel SiNWs@Co3O4 Z-scheme heterojunction photocathode with carbon quantum dots eco-friendly derived from sludge (SCQDs) as the co-catalyst. The photocathode not only leads to effective separation of electron–hole pair, lower transmission resistance, and longer lifetime of charge carriers, but also elevates the stability by preventing direct contact between the SiNWs and the electrolyte as well as the self-oxidation. Simultaneously, the excellent electron transport properties of the SCQDs further improved the PEC performance. Correspondingly, a maximum current density of 14.88 mA·cm−2 was obtained at −0.67 V with the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) achieving 8.4% under visible light irradiations at pH = 7. This work provides a promising scheme for Si-based photocathodes for PEC hydrogen production with a high performance, reliable stability, and low-cost.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, the scarcity of energy and environmental pollution are two major problems that require attentions due to their impacts on the lives of human and societal development (Tan et al., 2021; Wan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020b). Hydrogen is considered as a clean energy source with a considerable calorific value (Landman et al., 2017; Long et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2017). Therefore, it is promising as a future energy source that can effectively resolve the energy crisis and environmental contamination (Sharma et al., 2020). The Sun, which is the primary and inexhaustible source of all energy on the Earth, can be exploited in providing energy from hydrogen. To accomplish a large-scale solar-to‑hydrogen (STH) conversion, many approaches such as the photocatalytic (Boudjemaa et al., 2009; Cao et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020), electrocatalytic (Zhang et al., 2020a), and photoelectrochemical (PEC) (He et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2020) hydrogen production have been proposed. Among these hydrogen production methods, PEC hydrogen production has attracted much attention due to its high conversion efficiency, low system cost and independent hydrogen production (Li et al., 2021; Pang et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2020a). However, some challenges, for example, inadequate absorption and utilization of sunlight, difficulty in finding an appropriate band for the oxidation–reduction reaction, complications in charge separation on the surfaces of the electrode and electrolyte, and inability to ensure reasonable photostability during the PEC reaction, are retarding the feasible commercialization of this technology (Babu et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Muhmood et al., 2020). Consequently, Several semiconductor optical electrodes such as metal oxides (Wang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017b; Zeng et al., 2019), sulfides (Hassan et al., 2019), nitrides (Huang et al., 2019), oxynitrides (Feng et al., 2019), and phosphides (Britto et al., 2019) were developed and utilized to prepare photoelectrode, aiming at driving the oxidation and reduction of water, respectively.

Silicon (Si), the second most abundant crustal element, is an attractive material for the PEC photocathode because of its low cost and unique properties. Its bandgap (Eg) of 1.1 eV enables the absorption across most of the solar energy spectrum, and its conduction band is also suitable for hydrogen production (Hong et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2018a). One-dimensional (1D) silicon nanowires (SiNWs) can provide larger contact areas for reaction and promote efficient photogenerated carriers collection, thereby improving the charge collection efficiency (Sim et al., 2015). However, the limiting factors of SiNWs as ideal materials for PEC photocathodes are the speedy recombination of photogenerated electron hole pairs and the undesired photo-corrosion (Zhang et al., 2015).

To resolve these problems, integrating the SiNWs with other semiconductor materials to produce a Z-scheme heterojunction seems promising. In the Z-scheme heterojunction, the photogenerated electrons in the semiconductor with lower reducing power are combined with the photogenerated holes in the semiconductor with lower oxidizing power (Li et al., 2017a; Ren et al., 2019). In the Z-scheme photocatalyst, the redox ability can be optimized, the electrons and holes are effectively separated would be promoted. As a result, the PEC performance of the Z-scheme heterojunction has been enhanced in thermodynamics and kinetics. The Z-scheme heterojunction has been widely used in photocatalytic degradation (Wen et al., 2018), photocatalytic hydrogen production (Liao et al., 2021), photocatalytic CO2 reduction (Meng et al., 2018), etc. Cobalt tetroxide (Co3O4), for example, is a transition metal oxide with a bandgap of 2.2 eV, capable of absorbing visible light. The potential at the bottom of the Co3O4 conduction band edge (ECB = 0.37 V vs. NHE) slightly surpasses that at the top of the SiNWs valence band edge (EVB = 0.59 V vs. NHE) (Liu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018). Theoretically, Co3O4 represents a suitable material to combine with SiNWs for manufacturing an effective Z-scheme photocathode. In the Z-scheme heterojunction composite electrode of SiNWs and Co3O4, electrons accumulate on SiNWs and holes accumulate on Co3O4, Therefore, electrons and holes are effectively separated, thereby inhibiting the self-oxidation of silicon.

Concurrently, the fast electron transfer by photoexcited semiconductors is a vital property for efficient hydrogen production, which depends on the band position, interfaces, and electronic conductivity of semiconductors. Therefore, a high conductivity cocatalyst is essential to promote charge transfer and to improve the photohydrogen production activity. The carbon quantum dots are nanocarbons characterized by good electronic conductivity that have been utilized in recent years to promote the charge separation of photocatalysts (Feng et al., 2020; Li et al., 2017b; Meng et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020c). Many studies employed natural materials such as lemon juice (He et al., 2018), tea extract (Chen et al., 2019), banana juice (Chaudhry et al., 2020), mangifera indica leaves (Singh et al., 2020) and wheat straw (Chen et al., 2021) as the precursors for synthesizing CQDs. The excess sludge from sewage treatment plants can also be exploited as a carbon source for preparing CQDs, although this remains unpublished. The introduction of sludge derived CQDs is expected to promote the charge transfer and increase the stability of photocathodes.

In this study, a Z-scheme SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode was fabricated through chemical etching of a p-type Si (100) substrate, followed by a simple calcination and hydrothermal method. The Co3O4 can effectively weaken the self-oxidation of the SiNWs by forming Z-scheme heterojunctions, thereby acting as a protective layer preventing the direct contact between the SiNWs and electrolyte, and effectively retarding the corrosion of the SiNWs photoelectric electrode. The SCQDs were prepared and coated on the SiNWs@Co3O4 photocathode as a cocatalyst to enhance the hydrogen production performance and stability of the PEC. The morphology, crystal structure and photochemical characterization of the composite photocathode as well as the effects of the Co3O4 and SCQDs on the properties of the SiNWs under visible light were studied. Additionally, a mechanism for improving the PEC hydrogen production stability by the Z-scheme SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode is proposed.

SiNWs were fabricated by a one-step metal assisted chemical etching (MACE) method. Firstly, the silicon wafers were ultrasonicated in acetone, ethanol and deionized water for 10 min. This was then placed into a piranha solution (H2SO4:H2O2 of 3:1), washed with deionized water, and rinsed with HF (5%) to remove the oxide layer. The cleaned silicon wafer was immersed in a solution containing AgNO3 (0.02 M) and HF (4.6 M) for different times, and then submerged in HNO3 for an hour to remove residual Ag particles on the Si wafer. After cleaning, the silicon wafers were immersed in solutions containing AgNO3 (0.02 M) and HF (4.6 M), at 50°C for different times (20, 40, 60, and 80 min), and then immersed in dilute nitric acid for 1 h to remove the residual Ag particles on the silicon wafers.

Excess sludge was collected from the secondary sedimentation tank of the Qingdao Tuandao sewage treatment plant. Approximately 30 mL of sludge was mixed with 50 mL of PTFE lining, and the mixture was retained in a high-pressure reactor at 200°C for 9 h. After cooling of the reactor to room temperature, the product was filtered and the filtrate dialyzed for three days in a dialysis bag. The dialysate was changed every 8 h and the SCQDs were obtained by drying at 60°C in an oven.

The particle size distribution of the sludge-derived carbon quantum dots prepared by the method is uniform (Fig. S1), with the average particle size is about 2 nm. The sludge-derived carbon quantum dots have good photoluminescence properties (Fig. S2).

The oxide layer on the surface of prepared SiNWs photocathode was removed with 5% HF solution. This was followed by the immersion in ethanol solutions with different Co3O4 concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 M) for 30 min, and stirred continuously. After drying with nitrogen, the SiNWs were immediately transferred to a muffle furnace and decomposed for 2 h at 400°C to obtain the Z-scheme SiNWs@Co3O4 heterojunction.

The prepared SiNWs@Co3O4 heterojunction was then immersed in the ethanol solution containing different concentrations of SCQDs (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100 mg·L−1), and transferred into the high-pressure reactor with a 50 mL PTFE lining for 3 h at 100°C. The SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode was obtained by drying with nitrogen after the high-pressure returned to room temperature. The preparation of SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . In subsequent experiments, unless otherwise specified, the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode represents the photocathode prepared with a 0.3 M Co3O4 precursor solution and 50 mg·L−1 SCQDs.

The SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode with a surface area of 4 cm2 was connected to a titanium wire using a conductive adhesive and sealed with epoxy resin for insulation from the electrolyte. The PEC water electrolysis was performed in a three-electrode configuration with a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China), the electrolyte is 0.5 M Na2SO4 (pH = 7). The photocathode, Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire were used as working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode respectively. The Ag/AgCl measured potential was changed to the RHE level using the Nernst equation (Eq. (1)) expressed as:
(1)
E RHE
= E +
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.059 pH
where E
Ag/AgCl = 0.197 V, E is the measured potential relative to the Ag/AgCl reference, and the pH of the electrolyte is 7. All measurements were conducted under a 100 mW·cm−2 intensity of AM 1.5G sunlight. The frequency extent of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements was 0.1 Hz ~ 1 MHz, with an AC amplitude of 5 mV (Dong et al., 2019). In the dark, the Mott–Schottky analysis was performed in a three-electrode system at a frequency of 1 kHz (Wang et al., 2019).

The effects of the etching time on the SiNWs morphology are illustrated in Fig. S3, with only 1–2 μm nano blocks growing on the Si surface in the first 20 min (Fig. S3a). As the etching time was extended to 40 min, the linear structures began appearing (Fig. S3b). The nanowire structure emerged on the Si wafer with the nanowire growing vertically as the etching time reached 60 min (Fig. S3c). As the etching time was extended further, nanowires could not maintain the array due to their mechanical strength (Fig. S3d), which was unfavorable for subsequent investigation. Therefore, 60 min was chosen as the etching time. The micro morphology of the pristine SiNWs (length: 4 μm) reveals aggregation at the top due to the electrostatic effect and capillary action (Fig. S4a) and vertical arrangement on the silicon substrate (Fig. S4b). The surface morphology and crystal structure of the SiNWs and SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode samples were examined using TEM and HR-TEM. The SiNWs is shown in Fig. 2a, with the lattice fringes d of 0.329 nm (Fig. 2b) corresponding to the (100) crystal face of Si (Choi et al., 2020). For the TEM image of SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode in Fig. 2c, Co3O4 displays uniform coating on the SiNWs surface, with the black dots representing SCQDs. Further, the HR-TEM image of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode in Fig. 2d shows that the lattice fringes of SiNWs are wrapped with Co3O4 and doped with SCQDs. The lattice fringes d of 0.243 nm is attributed to the (311) crystal faces of Co3O4 (Lu et al., 2019), respectively, based on calculations from Fig. 2d. The lattice spacing d of 0.205 nm is attributed to the graphene (102) crystal surface of the SCQDs (Zhang et al., 2016). Due to the coexistence of Si, Co, and C, the Si is characterized by a lower intensity peak in the EDS spectrum of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode (Fig. S5). In addition, the Co, O, and C overlap with Si, as depicted in Fig. 2e–i, indicating that the Co3O4 and SCQDs are adequately deposited on the SiNWs photoelectrode. The core-shell structure of the composite photoelectrode shown in Fig. S6 could prevent direct contact between the SiNWs and the electrolyte, thereby effectively retarding the otherwise rapid corrosion of the SiNWs photoelectrode.

XRD patterns for Co3O4, SiNWs, and the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode are displayed in Fig. 3a. In the XRD pattern of Co3O4, pronounced peaks appear at 18.99°, 31.27°, 36.85°, 44.80°, 59.35°, and 65.23°, which are attributed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) crystal planes, respectively (Lu et al., 2019). The SiNWs were orientated along the (400) crystal plane characteristic (JCPDS card No: 75-0589) of a cubic crystal structure (Hu et al., 2020). For the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode, Co3O4 diffraction peaks are only observed at 18.99°, 36.85°, 59.35°, and 65.23°, corresponding to the (111), (311), (511), and (440) crystal planes, respectively, maybe due to the extremely high Si diffraction peak intensity. After coating the SiNWs with Co3O4, the Si (400) peak shifts slightly to the left (illustration of Fig. 3a), confirming the strong interaction between the SiNWs and Co3O4, which indicates the formation of a hybrid heterojunction structure. XRD patterns suggest that SCQDs is amorphous. Therefore, there is no diffraction pattern of SCQDs on the XRD pattern of SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode.

The XPS full survey spectrum for the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode is shown in Fig. 3b. The binding energies at 99, 530, 780, and 284 eV are characteristic peaks corresponding to the Si 2p, O 1s, Co 2p, and C 1s, respectively. The two peaks at 99.2 and 102.0 eV in the Si 2p spectrum correspond to Si and SiO2 (Yang et al., 2019), whereas the peak at 105.6 eV is attributed to the SiN bond in the SCQDs (Fig. 3c). Also, the XPS spectra for the SCQDs in Fig. S7 reveal that the prepared SCQDs are N-doped (Di et al., 2016). Disposal of the large amount of surplus sludge from domestic sewage plants has always been a challenge. The preparation of sludge derived carbon quantum dots not only reduces the excess sludge, but also transforms the excess sludge into useful electrode materials, which is a new way of sludge disposal and has a positive effect on environmental protection. The two diffraction peaks at 529.5 and 531.0 eV in the O 1s spectrum are assigned to the Co3O4 and SiO2/C=O bands, respectively (Fig. 3d) (Di et al., 2016). The satellite features of the Co 2p spectrum in Fig. 3e are due to the presence of Co(II) and Co(III) in the Co3O4 (Tang et al., 2017). Similarly, the C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3f reflects the CC bond and C–O/C–N bonds of the 284.5 and 286.0 eV diffraction peaks (Di et al., 2016). The migration of electrons on the semiconductor surface will cause a shift in the binding energy. A positive shift in the binding energy indicates a decrease in the electron density, and a negative shift indicates an increase in the electron density. In compared with the XPS spectra of SiNWs and Co3O4 photocathodes (Figs. S8 and S9), the Si 2p binding energy and Co 2p binding energy for the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode have a negative and positive shift, respectively, indicating electron transfer from Co3O4 to SiNWs. Therefore, the Z-scheme heterojunction between SiNWs and Co3O4 is confirmed.

The PEC performance of photocathodes with different SCQDs loadings were evaluated by the LSV measurements (Fig. S10). The introduction of SCQDs significantly increases the photocurrent of the SiNWs@Co3O4 composite photocathode, with an optimized SCQDs concentration of 50 mg·L−1. Increased loading of SCQDs from 50 to 100 mg·L−1 may affect the light absorption of the photocathode and influence the performance of the composite photocathode. Therefore, the SCQDs concentration of 50 mg·L−1 was used in all the other experiments in this work.

The PEC hydrogen production performance of the prepared composite photocathodes was then evaluated by LSV measurements under visible light with an AM 1.5G filter. As shown in Fig. 4a, the hybrid heterojunction photocathode significantly improves the photocurrent density compared with the SiNWs photocathode under illumination, while the dark voltammetry measurement involves a low current density. The improved LSV performance of SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs further demonstrates the superiority of the Co3O4 and SCQDs as the interface layer of the SiNWs photocathode, with a solar-induced water reduction performance. In the optical current density–voltage (J–V) measurement process, a large number of bubbles evolves from the photocathode, representing direct evidence of the HER hydrogen production. The composite heterojunction photocathode shows an amazing performance improvement in J–V diagram, and this is attributed to the high photocurrent density and the Co3O4/SCQDs as the interface layer. The photocurrent density of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs composite heterostructure photocathode increased with the increase of the concentration of Co3O4 precursor solution from 0.1 to 0.3 M and then decreased with further increase of Co3O4 concentration from 0.3 to 0.5 M. A maximum photocurrent density of 14.88 mA·cm−2 at −0.67 V vs. RHE was obtained at 0.3 M Co3O4 precursor solution. With lower concentrations of Co3O4 precursor, the photoelectrochemical activity of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs composite photocathode was lower primarily due to less distribution and heteroconjunction of Co3O4 with SiNWs. When the Co3O4 concentration increases to 0.3 M, a relatively uniform Co3O4 layer was formed on the SiNWs with rugged surface structure (Fig. S11) which protects the SiNWs from self-oxidation and provides more catalytic active sites. With further increase in the concentration of Co3O4, however, the excessive accumulation of Co3O4 on the surface of the SiNWs@Co3O4 photocathode may decrease the surface active sites of SiNWs, hinder the light absorption by SiNWs and lead to the decrease of catalytic performance with increased Co3O4 nanoparticles (Han et al., 2017). The Co3O4, acting as the interface layer, strongly influenced the PEC process performance, mainly because the Co3O4 suppresses the carrier recombination life at the composite heterostructure photocathode and electrolyte interface. Moreover, introducing the SCQDs as a cocatalyst can effectively increase the electron transfer rate and improve the water decomposition performance of the PEC process (Wang et al., 2017a). Here, the Co3O4/SCQDs enhances the PEC hydrogen production by suppressing the recombination lifetime of charge carriers of the composite heterostructure at the photocathode/electrolyte interface. Notably, the initial potential of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode proton reduction process shifts positively (875 mV vs. RHE), surpassing that of the SiNWs photocathode (545 mV vs. RHE), likely due to the rapid separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The short-circuit current density (JSC) of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode at 0 V vs. RHE is 1.59 mA·cm−2. Compared with the SiNWs photocathode, the JSC of the hybrid photocathode is higher, because of its better optical absorption and carrier transport performance. Overall, the enhanced light absorption and accelerated charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface which is mainly attributed to the presence of Co3O4 and SCQDs on the SiNWs composite photocathode significantly promoted its PEC performance.

The photocathode stability was measured by chronoamperometry (J–t) using a 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte under 0 V vs. RHE bias and 100 mW·cm−2 illumination for 4 h. The J–t curve for the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs composite photocathode prepared with a 0.3 M Co3O4 precursor solution in Fig. 4b displays excellent stability. Compared with the EDS characterization of the photocathode before the reaction (Fig. S5), there is almost no leakage of Co and C on the photocathode after the reaction (Fig. S12). Meanwhile, the XRD and XPS results of the composite photocathode after reaction with insignificant changes were shown in Figs. S13 and S14, indicating that the composite photocathode had good stability. This is because the surface of the SiNWs is evenly covered by the Co3O4, which prevents direct contact between the SiNWs and the electrolyte during the PEC water decomposition (Hu et al., 2018b). Simultaneously, due to the Z-scheme heterojunction, the Co3O4 photogenerated electrons and the SiNWs photogenerated holes can quickly compound in the PEC process, thereby preventing the SiNWs self-oxidation process. Meanwhile, the SCQDs can increase the conductivity of the electrode, and this is conducive for generating electrons for the PEC hydrogen production process (Roy et al., 2020). In contrast, the SiNWs photocathode produces a lower photocurrent at 0 V vs. RHE (Fig. 4a), which is explained by the poor charge separation and autoxidation of nanowires because of direct contact with the electrolyte. Concurrently, the theoretical hydrogen production under a bias voltage of 0 V vs. RHE was calculated according to Faraday's law of electrolysis in Eq. (2). The theoretically estimated H2 evolution rate is 13.5 μmol·h−1·cm−2 for the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode.

The electrolysis equation is expressed as follows:
(2)
Mole
H 2
=
∫ 0 t
Idt / 2 F
where F is the Faraday constant (coulomb/one mole), I denotes the photocurrent density (mA), and t represents the time (h).

The photoconversion efficiency termed ABPE for evaluating the PEC hydrogen production performance of the prepared composite heterojunction photocathode is obtained from Eq. (S1). The photoconversion efficiency of the composite and the SiNWs photocathodes based on the applied voltages are displayed in Fig. 4c illustration. The ABPE of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode prepared with a 0.3 M Co3O4 precursor solution is 8.4% at −0.67 V vs. RHE, which is 2.7 times higher than that of the original SiNWs photocathode (3.1% at −0.67 V vs. RHE). The PEC performance of the prepared composite photocathode is better than that of the silicon-based photocathode reported (Table S1). Additionally, the STH efficiency was calculated using Eq. (3). As shown in Fig. 4c, the STH for the composite photocathode prepared with a 0.3 M Co3O4 precursor solution is also the maximum (0.07%), while that for the SiNWs photocathode is just 0.01%. The expression for obtaining the STH is given as:
(3)
STH
%
=
J sc
× 1.23 ×
η F
/
P light
where JSC is the short-circuit photocurrent density (mA·cm−2), the constant 1.23 is the standard hydrogen production potential, ηF is the Faraday efficiency, considered as 100%, and Plight is the incident light intensity (mW·cm−2).

The improved performance may be related to the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole. Therefore, the separation rate of the photoinduced charge carriers for the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathodes was assessed using the photoluminescence (PL) curves (Xiao et al., 2020b) in Fig. 4d. The SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode exhibits lower PL signals as compared to the SiNWs and SiNWs@Co3O4 photocathode. The SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode prepared with a 0.3 M Co3O4 precursor solution showed the lowest PL intensity because of the suitable loading of Co3O4. The lower peak intensity is caused by the less recombination of the charge carriers, with the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathodes displaying better photoinduced charge separation.

The electrochemical kinetic characteristics of the composite heterojunction photocathodes were further evaluated from EIS measurements, including charge carrier separation and recombination lifetime at the substrate/electrolyte interface. The Nyquist plots for the composite and SiNWs photocathodes were measured under solar irradiations at open circuit voltages and the test results were fitted to two equivalent circuits are displayed Fig. 5a. Generally, the Nyquist diagram for the composite heterojunction photocathode exhibits two arcs in the low frequency and intermediate frequency regions. Rs represents the equivalent series resistance, and is affected by the internal resistance of the optical electrode and the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte, with the CPE1 and CPE2 as the constant phase elements, and revealing the interface charge transfer resistance at the photocathode/electrolyte (R1) and SiNWs/Co3O4–SCQDs (R2) interfaces. The Rs for the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode prepared with a 0.3 M Co3O4 precursor solution is 9.44 Ω, which is lower than the values for other composite electrodes, whereas the Rs for the SiNWs photocathode is 21 Ω, higher than those of all composite photocathodes (Table S2). The higher Rs for the SiNWs photocathode is explained by conductivity improvement associated with its Co3O4/SCQDs coating. However, the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode yields the most appropriate conductivity for reducing the resistance of the electrochemical reaction, thus promoting the water decomposition. Compared with the SiNWs photocathode, the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathodes display low interface charge transfer resistances and small semicircle radius, which can enhance the electron–hole pairs separation. This enhancement can be closely related to the internal electric field at the SiNWs@Co3O4 heterojunction and high electron transfer of the SCQDs. These promote photo induced carrier separation by reducing the interfacial barrier potential of electrochemical reactions.

In addition, the carrier lifetime of the photocathode can be calculated according to the Bode diagram of the photocathodes (Fig. 5b) using Eq. (4) as follows:
(4)
τ = 1 / 2 π
f max
where τ is carrier life time and f
max is the frequency of the maximum peak.

The fmax of the composite photocathode decreases from 213.8 to 21.4 Hz, demonstrating that the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs heterojunction photocathode improves the charge separation and the interface charge transfer. However, the photocathode produced using a high concentration precursor solution is unfavorable for charge transfer, which is consistent with the photocurrent analysis results. Compared with the τ of 0.7 ms for the SiNWs photocathode, that of the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode significantly increases to 7.4 ms. These results demonstrate that the composite photocathode with the SCQDs cocatalyst exhibits advantages including effective electron–hole pair separation, small interfacial charge transfer resistance, and longer charge carrier lifetime, which improve the PEC hydrogen production performance.

To further get insight into the intrinsic properties of the photocathode, the Mott–Schottky diagram is shown in Fig. 6a. The scanning potentials of the SiNWs and composite photocathodes measured under dark conditions range from −0.3 to 1.1 V vs. RHE. In Fig. 6a, all tangent lines show negative slopes, indicating that the photocathode is a p-type semiconductor. The flat band potential (EFB) is calculated by extrapolating the x-axis intercept (capacitance (C−2)) of the linear area in the Mott–Schottky diagram. The SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs and SiNWs@Co3O4 photocathode have similar flat band potentials. The introduction of SCQDs will not change the band gap of the composite electrode. The EFB of 1.09 V vs. RHE for the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs photocathode is significantly higher than that for the SiNWs photocathode (0.52 V vs. RHE). The enhanced PEC activity of the composite photocathode is attributed to the synergistic effect of the SiNWs and Co3O4. The two possible heterojunctions (Fig. 6b) are: (1) the type II heterojunction and (2) the Z-scheme heterojunction. If the mechanism of the heterojunction of the photocathode is type II, then under the illumination condition, the Co3O4 photogenerated holes should be transferred to the SiNWs valence band by more negative potentials, with no positive shift in its flat band potential. The Z- scheme heterojunction composite photoelectrode with a more negative potential could promote the separation of electrons and holes. And the current density, the adsorption of sacrificial agent, charge separation and electron transfer would be promoted compared to type II heterojunction due to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction of SiNWs & Co3O4, resulting in better PEC performance. Moreover, in the conventional mechanism, the photogenerated electrons of the SiNWs will be transferred to the Co3O4 conduction band, thereby weakening the reduction performance. This is unfavorable for hydrogen generation, contrary to the experimental results. As shown in Fig. 6a, the flat band potential of the composite photocathode shifts forward by 0.5 V vs. RHE, proving that the structure of the SiNWs and Co3O4 characterizes a Z-scheme heterojunction. In order to directly prove the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction, 7-hydroxycoumarin was used for hydroxyl capture experiment under illumination. As shown in Fig. S15, the increase in the fluorescence intensity of 7-hydroxycoumarin proves the generation of hydroxyl radicals. The composite photoelectrode can generate hydroxyl radicals only when a Z-scheme heterojunction is formed. These experimental results are consistent with the results of XPS analysis, which proves that SiNWs and Co3O4 form the Z-scheme heterojunction. When the SCQDs are introduced in the composite electrode, the photogenerated electrons produced by the Co3O4 conduction band are rapidly transferred to the surface of the SiNWs, and these compound with the photogenerated holes.

These results further demonstrate that the composite photocathode improves the PEC water decomposition performance. The possible mechanism for improving the charge separation and electrons transfer for the composite photocathode is displayed in Fig. 6b. As shown in Fig. 7 , high density photoelectrons and holes are produced under light irradiation. The photoelectrons are converted to H2 through the reduction of H+, while the holes combine with electrons from the Pt electrode. Generally, the energy band structure of the substrate and the composite catalyst in the interface layer is the decisive factor in the PEC water reduction process. An additional barrier is generated at the heterojunction/electrolyte interface due to the discontinuity of SiNWs and Co3O4 in the heterojunction. In the SiNWs@Co3O4/SCQDs composite heterojunction photocathode, interface dipoles reduce the onset potential of the hydrogen production process and extend the life of the light induced charge carriers. These interfacial dipoles limit the electrochemical reaction potential by opposing the extra barrier in the prepared photocathode, owing to the continuity produced in the conduction band (CB) edges. As discussed above, the Z-scheme heterojunction between SiNWs and Co3O4 successfully formed in the prepared composite photocathode, which was verified by the XPS, Mott-Schottky, and hydroxyl radical capture experiment, could enhance the redox potential and the charge separation, resulting in significant increase in its PEC performance. Moreover, the transfer of photogenerated electrons from Co3O4 to SiNWs as well as the formative of Co3O4 layer on SiNWs could inhibit the photo-oxidation of SiNWs, improving the stability of the photocathode. Meanwhile, the doping of electrocatalyst SCQDs derived from excess sludge onto SiNWs@Co3O4 not only promoted the electron transfer of photocathode for hydrogen production, but also provides opportunities for the resource utilization of excess sludge.

In summary, we designed a new Z-scheme heterojunction photocathode system comprising SiNWs, Co3O4, and excess SCQDs. The system was demonstrated to be competing for high PEC hydrogen production performance at a neutral pH. At −0.67 V vs. RHE, the maximum photocurrent density and ABPE efficiency obtained under visible light irradiation are 14.88 mA·cm−2 and 8.4%, respectively, with a significant change in the initial potential of anode. The EIS results showed that Co3O4/SCQDs injection into the interface layer effectively induced separation and transfer of electron–hole pairs and extended the carrier lifetime, thus improving the conversion efficiency. The Z-scheme design also effectively inhibited the self-oxidation of the silicon substrate and increased the PEC stability of the photocathode (stable operation for 4 h). The SCQDs coated on the Z-scheme photocathode as cocatalyst enhanced the PEC performance under visible light irradiations, with a theoretical H2 is. 13.5 μmol·h−1·cm−2. This work provides a stable and high performance photocathode for PEC hydrogen production.
